coredata.dc:title: Enhancement in the fluorescence of graphene quantum dots by hydrazine hydrate reduction
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2013 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Feng, Yaqiang
Zhao, Jinping
Yan, Xingbin
Tang, Fuling
Xue, Qunji
coredata.dc:description: A simple and effective chemical method was reported to enhance the fluorescence of graphene quantum dots (GQDs). Specifically, water-soluble GQDs, prepared by solvothermal synthesis from graphene oxide, are chemically reduced by hydrazine hydrate to produce reduced GQDs (rGQDs). The results show that the hydrazine hydrate reduction not only decreases the O/C atomic ratio of GQDs, also changes the bonding type of N atoms. Such surface/edge chemical bond change of GQDs results in that as-made rGQDs exhibit more than two times fluorescence intensity as strong as that of the pristine GQDs.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of graphene oxide (GO) and GQDs
Chemical reduction of GQDs with hydrazine hydrate

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of GQDs, (b) high-resolution TEM image of an individual GQD, (c) the size distribution of GQDs, (d) AFM image of GQDs and (e) the height profile along the lines in (d). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

The optical properties of GQDs before and after hydrazine hydrate reduction: (a) UV-vis and (b) PL spectra with different hydrazine hydrate doses and the same reaction time of 60min and (c) UV-vis and (d) PL spectra with the same hydrazine hydrate dose of 2μL and different reaction times. The PL spectra were obtained for excitation at 320nm. Insets of (a) and (c) are magnified absorbance curves from red dashed circles. Insets of (b) and (d) are shown the relationship between PL intensity (420nm) and the amount of hydrazine hydrate, PL intensity (420nm) and reduction time, respectively. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

XPS survey spectra (a) and FTIR spectra (d) of GQDs and rGQDs, well-fitted C1s XPS spectra of GQDs (b) and rGQDs (e) and well-fitted N1s XPS spectra of GQDs (c) and rGQDs (f). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Scheme of chemical structure changes of GQDs before and after hydrazine hydrate reduction.
XPS data of GQDs and rGQDs.

head.ce:title: Enhancement in the fluorescence of graphene quantum dots by hydrazine hydrate reduction

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory of Clean Energy Chemistry and Materials, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Lanzhou 730000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Laboratory of Clean Energy Chemistry and Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Solid Lubrication, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Lanzhou 730000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Solid Lubrication

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Gansu Advanced Non-Ferrous Metal Materials, Department of Material Science and Engineering, Lanzhou University of Technology, Lanzhou 730050, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Gansu Advanced Non-Ferrous Metal Materials
Department of Material Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple and effective chemical method was reported to enhance the fluorescence of graphene quantum dots (GQDs). Specifically, water-soluble GQDs, prepared by solvothermal synthesis from graphene oxide, are chemically reduced by hydrazine hydrate to produce reduced GQDs (rGQDs). The results show that the hydrazine hydrate reduction not only decreases the O/C atomic ratio of GQDs, also changes the bonding type of N atoms. Such surface/edge chemical bond change of GQDs results in that as-made rGQDs exhibit more than two times fluorescence intensity as strong as that of the pristine GQDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene has attracted tremendous attention because of its novel and unique properties for many potential applications in scientific and technological fields [1–3]. However, graphene is a zero-bandgap semiconductor, which limits its electronic and opto-electronic applications [4]. For example, due to the lack of a bandgap in graphene, the possibility of observing luminescence seems highly unlikely [5]. Thus, many efforts for opening a bandgap in graphene to induce photoluminescence (PL) have been done through controlling the size and surface chemistry of graphene. Recently, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been verified most likely as a fluorescent graphene material due to their strong quantum confinement and edge effects [6].

To date, two typical approaches, bottom-up oxidative condensation reactions and top-down cutting of graphene sheets, have been exploited to prepare colloidal GQDs [7]. For the former [8–10], GQDs are generally synthesized by oxidative condensation of aryl groups in liquid-phase reaction. It involves complex synthesis steps and is difficult for mass production. In comparison, the later approach refers to the cutting of graphene sheets into ultra-small GQDs and is relatively simple and effective. Recently, colloidal GQDs have been fabricated by cutting sp2 clusters in graphene sheets through hydrothermal route [11,12], and green-luminescent GQDs have been obtained by electrochemical route [13,14], and strongly fluorescent GQDs with PL quantum yield as high as 11.4% have also been prepared by solvothermal method [15,16]. These as-prepared GQDs contain oxygen-rich functional groups (epoxy and hydroxyl groups on the basal plane or carboxylic groups on edges), and some groups usually result in non-radiative recombination of localized electron-hole pairs [16]. Therefore, it is expected that the fluorescence characteristics of GQDs could be efficiently adjusted and controlled by changing the surface/edge chemical bonds of GQDs.

To enhance the luminescence of carbon-based fluorescent materials, including carbon dots and emerging GQDs, surface passivation and chemical reduction are the most common pathways [17–22]. For GQDs, Shen et al. prepared GQDs with surface passivation by an oligomeric PEG diamine into ultrafine GQDs with strong blue emission [20]. Zhu et al. and Li et al. obtained reduced GQDs by NaBH4 reducing and the PL quantum yield is two times higher than that for the pristine GQDs [16,21]. Recently, Sun et al. developed a new pathway to prepare bright blue GQDs with a high quantum yield of 8.8% through photo-reducing [22]. However, the major challenge is how to effective reduction (choose suitable reductant and explore new pathways). Further, it also needs to clarify the mystery that reduction process is how to improve the fluorescence of GQDs.

Herein, we attempt to manipulate the surface/edge chemical groups of GQDs by liquid-phase hydrazine hydrate reduction for tailoring their optical properties. Interestingly, as-made chemically reduced GQDs (rGQDs) exhibits much stronger PL intensity compared with pristine GQDs. So the relationship between the enhancement of PL intensity and the change of surface/edge chemical groups is studied in detail.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of graphene oxide (GO) and GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: GO was synthesized from natural graphite powder by a modified Hummers method [23,24]. The detailed synthesis procedures can also be found in our previous report [25]. GQDs were synthesized from GO by one-step solvothermal method [15–16,26]. In a typical synthesis, GO (540mg) was firstly suspended in N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF, 40mL) with the aid of sonication (120W, 40kHz) for 30min. After that, the mixture was transferred to a poly-(tetrafluoroethylene) autoclave (60mL) and heated up to 200°C for 8h. After being cooled to room temperature, the mixture was filtered using a 0.22μm microporous membrane and the brown filter solution was collected. The suspension of GQDs in DMF is obtained and stable. The DMF solvent of the brown solution was removed using a rotary evaporator, and the residue was then dispersed in ultrapure water and purified by dialysis. The powdery GQDs were obtained by lyophilisation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical reduction of GQDs with hydrazine hydrate
ce:section.ce:para: The aqueous solution of GQDs (10mL, 0.18mg/mL) was added in a 20mL glass vial. After adding hydrazine hydrate (85 wt% in water) with different volumes (0.5, 1, 2, 10 or 30μL) and being vigorously stirred for a few minutes, the vial was put in an oil bath at 95°C for refluxing with different reaction times (20s, 1, 3, 10, 30 or 60min). After rapidly cooling down to room temperature, excess hydrazine hydrate was removed by dialysis. The powdery rGQDs were also obtained by lyophilisation. (Note: In order to confirm excess hydrazine hydrate having completely removed, we monitored the dialysate in which 30μL hydrazine hydrate was added. Through comparing the absorption spectra between the dialysate and pure hydrazine hydrate, we found that the excess hydrazine hydrate can be removed completely after 3days dialysis.)

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained on a FEI TECNAT G2 transmission electron microscope at an accelerating voltage of 200kV. The test sample was obtained by drop-casting the GQDs aqueous dispersion on a carbon-coated copper grid, followed by drying at room temperature. Atomic force microscopic (AFM) image was obtained on a Multimode 8 atomic force microscope. The sample was prepared by drop-casting the GQDs dispersion on a clean silicon wafer and dried at 60°C in the oven. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses were carried out on an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (ESCALAB250xi) with an Al K Alpha as the X-ray source. The pass energy was set at 200eV for survey scans and 50eV for high-resolution scans were used. The sample was prepared as the same as AFM sample. Binding energy calibration was based on C1s at 284.6eV. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra were obtained on a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (IFS120HR) using KBr pellets. UV-vis absorption spectra (Abs) of GQDs in water were recorded on a Hitachi U-3010 UV-vis spectrophotometer. PL spectra were obtained by a F-4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer.

In our system, the fluorescence color of the solution of GQDs changes from blue to yellowish-green when GQDs are transferred to the water from DMF (See supporting information, Fig. S1). This is due to the increase of the solvent polarity, leading to decrease the π–π ∗ transition energy, and so that the fluorescence spectrum happens red shift. The GQDs are highly soluble in water, and the suspension is very homogeneous without any perceptible change even after being placed at 4°C for 5months.

Fig. 1 a shows a typical TEM image of GQDs synthesized by one-step solvothermal method. These GQDs are well-dispersed and show a relatively narrow size distribution ranging from 2nm to 5.5nm with an average diameter of 3.8nm (Fig. 1c). Fig. 1b displays a representative high-resolution TEM image of an individual GQD. The distinct crystal lattice indicates the crystallinity of GQD and the lattice parameter of 0.242nm represents the (1120) lattice fringe of graphene [4,27]. Moreover, AFM image shown in Fig. 1d demonstrates the topographic morphology of GQDs, and the heights of which are between 0.6nm and 2nm (Fig. 1e), corresponding to 1–3 graphene layers [13].

As shown in Fig. 2 a, with the increase of hydrazine hydrate dose, the main UV-vis absorbance peak slightly red shifts from 195 to 205nm (Inset of Fig. 2a), while absorbance value increases first and then decreases, reaches a maximal value when adding 2μL hydrazine hydrate. This absorbance peak is attributed to π–π ∗ transition of CC in as-synthesized GQDs, suggesting that the electronic conjugation with GQDs is restored upon hydrazine reduction [2,5]. Interestingly, compared with the pristine GQDs, all rGQDs display a new and weak peak at about 270nm (Fig. 2a and c), which may be caused by the surface change of GQDs after reduction. Similar to previous reports [15], our GQDs and rGQDs exhibit an excitation-dependent behavior (See supporting information, Fig. S2). The corresponding PL spectra are shown in Fig. 2b. It is clear seen that, after chemical reduction, the PL intensity is significantly enhanced, while the PL peak position remains constant around ∼430nm when excited at 320nm wavelength. When the dose of hydrazine hydrate is 2μL, the reduced GQDs shows a strong PL intensity, which is more than two times as strong as that for the pristine GQDs. When the dose of hydrazine hydrate exceeds 2μL, the PL intensity has no significant enhancement (Inset of Fig. 2b). As we known, hydrazine hydrate is an environment hazardous chemical reagent. Therefore, the optimal dose of hydrazine hydrate is chosen as 2μL (the weight ratio
R
N
2
H
4
/ GQDs
= 0.624
). Fig. 2c shows the UV-vis adsorption change of GQDs after 2μL hydrazine hydrate reduction with different reaction times. It can be clearly seen that the absorbance value sharply increases after the hydrazine hydrate reduction. However, for reduced GQDs samples with different reduction times, there is no obvious change in absorbance intensity. The corresponding PL spectra (Fig. 2d) show that, with the increase of the reduction time, the PL intensity gradually increases until the reduction time is up to 30min (Inset of Fig. 2d).

It should be mentioned that, there is no obvious change in the size or height of GQDs after reduction reaction with hydrazine hydrate (See supporting information, Fig. S3 and S4), indicating that the change of the fluorescence properties of GQDs could be attributed to their surface chemical change after reduction rather than their dimension variation. Therefore, we further characterized the structure change of the GQDs before and after reduction (
R
N
2
H
4
/ GQDs
is 0.624 and the reduction time is 30min, marked as rGQDs). XPS and FTIR measurements were carried out to probe the chemical compositions of GQDs and rGQDs. As presented in Fig. 3 a, for the GQDs and rGQDs samples, the XPS survey spectra both show three dominant peaks: C1s peak at ca. 284.6eV, O1s peak at ca. 532eV and N1s peak at ca. 400eV. After hydrazine reduction, the O/C atomic ratio drops from 0.78 to 0.41, indicating that hydrazine reduction restores the electronic conjugation of GQDs [2]. However, after reducing by hydrazine hydrate, reduced GQDs still high oxygen content. This may be related to the size and the edge effects of GQDs. GQDs have much higher atom ratio between edge carbons and platelet carbons. The oxygen-containing groups at edges (such as carboxyl groups, –COOH) cannot be reduced totally by hydrazine hydrate [16]. Meanwhile, the N/C atomic ratio also decreases from 0.19 to 0.08, indicating that hydrazine reduction can partly remove initial nitrogen-containing groups of GQDs. Fig. 3b and e show the well-fitted C1s XPS spectra of GQDs and rGQDs, respectively. Each C1s XPS spectrum can be divided into five different peaks, which correspond to the signals of C–C (284.6eV), C–N (286±0.2eV), C–O (286.8±0.2eV), CO (288±0.2eV), and O–CO (289±0.2eV). The corresponding analytic results are summarized in Table 1 . As shown in XPS data, after chemical reduction, the C–N percentage decreases from 27.39% to 22.86% and the C–O percentage decreases from 13.34% to 7.74%. Such structure changes were further verified by FTIR spectra. As seen in Fig. 3d, the sample of GQDs shows phenolic hydroxyl group (3421 and 1099cm−1), carbonyl group (1637cm−1), carboxyl group (1408cm−1) and epoxide group (shoulder peak at 1024cm−1). The peaks at ca. 2440 and 1468cm−1 indicate the existence of the N-related bonds [14]. For rGQDs, the epoxide group disappears and the N-related bonds obviously become weak.

Fig. 3c and f show the well-fitted N1s XPS spectra of GQDs and rGQDs, which can be divided into two different peaks, a lower energy peak A (400.1±0.1eV) and a higher energy peak B (401.6±0.2eV). For both GQDs and rGQDs, peak B is well attributed to graphitic N atoms and peak A is suitably to pyrazole-N atoms. As the spectra show, the intensity ratio of I A/I B obviously alters, increases from 1.01 to 1.41, suggesting that N atoms are more likely formed pyrazole-N structure during hydrazine reduction process. It is known that pyrazole group can be synthesized by the reaction of 1,3-diketones with hydrazine [28] (as follow):

Moreover, Park et al. used isotopic labeling (13C and 15N) with NMR and XPS to study chemical structure of hydrazine treated graphene oxide, and they revealed the formation of five-membered pyrazoline ring or pyrazole ring at the platelet edges [29]. Pyrazoline derivatives have blue fluorescence emission with high quantum yield [30,31]. These two points well explain to the enhancement of PL after hydrazine reduction.

Based on the above analyses, we roughly describe the chemical structure of the GQDs and rGQDs (Fig. 4 ). The pristine GQDs, like GO, have relative high oxygen content with epoxy and –OH on the basal plane, –CON (CH3)2 and –COOH at the edges, also have diketone compounds at the edges. After reducing GQDs with hydrazine hydrate, epoxy groups are almost removed, and carboxyl moieties are partially reduced to hydroxyl group while pyrazole rings are formed at the platelet edges. Therefore, the fluorescence enhancement of GQDs by hydrazine hydrate reduction is mainly embodied in two aspects: on the one hand, the reduction restores the electronic conjugation on the basal planes of GQDs, and thereby reducing the non-radiative recombination of localized electron-hole pairs. On the other hand, pyrazole rings are formed at the edges of GQDs during the reduction process, which are beneficial to the PL emission. However, the excess of hydrazine hydrate might result in interconnectivity of GQDs, thereby facilitates hopping of excitons to nonradiative recombination centers, and consequently decrease absorbance value and PL intensity.

We have successfully used a simple hydrazine hydrate reduction to enhance the fluorescence of GQDs. The optimal reduction procedure (the weight ratio
R
N
2
H
4
/ GQDs
is approximately 0.6 and the reaction time is 30min) can increase PL intensity more than 2 times. By analyzing FTIR and XPS spectra of GQDs and rGQDs, we find that the surface chemical changes (conjugate repair and formed pyrazole rings) are the most important reason for the enhancement of PL intensity of GQDs.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Top Hundred Talents Program of the Chinese Academy of Sciences, the National Nature Science Foundations of China (51005225 and 51002161), and the Youth Science Foundations of Gansu Province (1107RJYA274). The authors thank Dr. Zhixin Tai for UV-vis and PL measurements guidance, Baoming Luo for the preparation graphene oxide guidance, Shoujun Zhu and Wenwen Liu for useful discussion and Mingshan Ding for AFM measurements.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2013.09.008.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Supplementary Figs. S1–S4 and Table S1.
